title: Quiz: Photon Detectors
description: Test your understanding of single-photon detector technologies and their performance metrics

questions.question: What does 'Geiger mode' mean for a Single-Photon Avalanche Diode (SPAD)?

options.text: The diode operates at very low temperature
The diode is biased above its breakdown voltage, enabling avalanche from single carriers
The diode can count the exact number of photons
The diode uses superconducting materials

questions.explanation: In Geiger mode, a SPAD is biased above its breakdown voltage. When a single photon generates a carrier, it triggers a self-sustaining avalanche that produces a large, detectable current. This must be actively quenched to reset the detector.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of SNSPDs over SPADs for quantum computing applications?

options.text: Room temperature operation
Lower cost
Near-unity detection efficiency (>95%) with very low dark counts (<10 Hz)
Ability to detect radio waves

questions.explanation: SNSPDs can achieve >98% detection efficiency with dark count rates below 10 Hz, far superior to SPADs. This makes them essential for high-fidelity photonic quantum computing despite requiring cryogenic operation at 0.8-4 K.

questions.question: Why is timing jitter important for photonic quantum computing?

options.text: It affects the speed of classical electronics
It can reduce Hong-Ou-Mandel interference visibility if comparable to photon coherence time
It determines the wavelength of detected photons
It only matters for pulsed laser sources

questions.explanation: Timing jitter adds uncertainty to photon arrival times. If this jitter is comparable to the photon coherence time, it can make photons partially distinguishable and reduce HOM interference visibility, degrading multi-photon gate fidelities.

questions.question: What is a Transition Edge Sensor (TES) and what capability does it provide?

options.text: A room-temperature detector with high speed
A superconducting detector operating at the transition temperature, providing photon number resolution
A semiconductor detector for infrared photons
A detector that only works with UV light

questions.explanation: A TES operates at the superconducting transition temperature where resistance is extremely sensitive to small energy changes. This allows it to distinguish the number of photons (0, 1, 2, 3...) based on the total absorbed energy, providing photon-number-resolving capability.

questions.question: What are 'dark counts' in single-photon detectors?

options.text: Counts from detecting infrared photons
False detection events that occur without any incident photons
Counts that happen in the dark part of the detector chip
Successful photon detections that go unrecorded

questions.explanation: Dark counts are false triggers that occur without any photon actually hitting the detector. They can arise from thermal carrier generation (SPADs), electrical noise, or background radiation. Dark counts limit detection fidelity and signal-to-noise ratio.

questions.question: Standard single-photon detectors (like basic SPADs or SNSPDs) can directly distinguish between 1, 2, or 3 photons arriving simultaneously.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False! Standard single-photon detectors are 'click' detectors - they produce a binary output (click or no click). They cannot distinguish photon number. Photon-number-resolving requires specialized detectors like TES, or spatial/temporal multiplexing of multiple detectors.

questions.question: For telecom wavelengths (1550 nm), which detector technology is typically required instead of silicon SPADs?

options.text: GaAs SPADs
InGaAs/InP SPADs
Germanium SPADs
Diamond SPADs

questions.explanation: Silicon is transparent to 1550 nm light (its bandgap corresponds to ~1100 nm cutoff). InGaAs/InP SPADs have a smaller bandgap and can detect telecom wavelengths, though with lower efficiency and higher dark counts than silicon SPADs.

questions.question: What is the typical operating temperature range for SNSPDs?

options.text: Room temperature (300 K)
Liquid nitrogen temperature (77 K)
0.8-4 K (requiring closed-cycle cryocoolers or dilution refrigerators)
-40 to 0 C

questions.explanation: SNSPDs require cryogenic temperatures to maintain superconductivity. They typically operate at 0.8-4 K, achievable with closed-cycle cryocoolers (for 4 K) or dilution refrigerators (for sub-kelvin operation with best performance).
